name: LocationServices
tagline: Integrate an on-device API: ask for the runtime location permission, then read the device's position from the fused location provider.
whatItDoes: You see a single screen titled 'Where am I?' that explains it reads your location through Google Play services. A status line shows whether the location permission is GRANTED (green) or NOT GRANTED (red). A button reads 'Grant permission & get location' the first time; tapping it pops the real Android system permission dialog for fine/coarse location. Once you allow it, the app fetches a fix and shows your latitude and longitude (to five decimals); the button becomes 'Refresh location'. While a fix is being acquired you see a spinner with 'Getting your location...'. By default it runs offline against a fake provider, so it returns a fixed coordinate after a short delay - the permission flow itself is fully real.
mentalModel: Reading the device location is two jobs: ask the user for a dangerous permission at run time (and handle them granting only coarse, only fine, or neither), then turn a callback-based Play services API into a clean suspend call whose result is one of Idle / Loading / Success / Error.

screens.name: LocationScreen
screens.role: Stateful entry composable: grabs the LocationViewModel, observes its uiState, tracks whether a location permission is held, builds the permission launcher, and decides whether tapping fetches or requests.

screens.name: LocationContent
screens.role: Stateless renderer: draws the explanation, the GRANTED/NOT GRANTED status, the current state (idle prompt, spinner, coordinate, or error), and the action button.

screens.name: LocationIdle/Loading/Success/Error previews
screens.role: Four @Preview functions that drive the stateless content with hand-made LocationUiState values and a fixed permission flag, no real Context.

concepts.plain: ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION and ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION are dangerous permissions: they need BOTH a <uses-permission> line in the manifest AND a run-time request in code. Declaring them is not enough - the user must approve them in a system dialog.

rememberLauncherForActivityResult with the RequestMultiplePermissions() contract requests fine and coarse together and hands back a Map<permission, granted>. The app treats ANY granted permission as enough, because the user may grant only the coarse (approximate) level.

The modern, battery-efficient device-location API. RealLocationRepository calls client.getCurrentLocation(PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY, token) to ask the hardware for a single fresh fix, then maps the framework Location into the app's own LocationData.

Play services exposes getCurrentLocation as a callback-based Task. suspendCancellableCoroutine wraps its success/failure listeners so callers just write 'val fix = repository.currentLocation()' - and cancelling the coroutine cancels the underlying request via a CancellationTokenSource.

LocationData is a plain data class with latitude and longitude and no Android imports. Keeping the framework's android.location.Location out of the ViewModel and UI means they never depend on Play services types and are trivial to fake in tests.

LocationUiState has exactly four cases: Idle (nothing requested yet), Loading (acquiring a fix), Success(location), and Error(message). Being sealed, a 'when' over it is exhaustive, so the UI is a pure function of state.

Unlike the networking demos, the ViewModel does NOT fetch in its init block. A location read must wait until permission is granted, so the screen calls fetchLocation() only after the launcher reports a grant (or when a permission is already held).

LocationRepository is an interface with one method currentLocation(). RealLocationRepository uses the GPS hardware; FakeLocationRepository returns a fixed coordinate after a delay. provideLocationRepository(context, useFake) is the single switch, defaulting to the offline fake so the app and tests run with no GPS.

files.role: The plain domain model (latitude, longitude) with no Android imports - the shape the ViewModel and UI use.

The LocationRepository interface plus RealLocationRepository (FusedLocationProviderClient, suspendCancellableCoroutine), FakeLocationRepository (offline), and provideLocationRepository(context, useFake) - the device/fake switch.
The LocationUiState sealed interface and the LocationViewModel that owns the StateFlow and the fetchLocation()/onPermissionDenied() logic.
The Compose UI: MainActivity, the stateful LocationScreen with its permission launcher, the stateless LocationContent, and four @Preview functions.
Declares ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION and ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION (step 1 of the permission model) and the MAIN/LAUNCHER intent-filter.
Project overview explaining the runtime-permission flow, the fused location provider, the callback-to-suspend bridge, and the Real-vs-Fake design.

highlights.title: Declaring the two location permissions (step 1)
highlights.explain: Both ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION (precise/GPS) and ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION (approximate/network) are declared. They are the static half of the two-step model - on Android 12+ the user may grant only the coarse level even when the app asks for fine, so a well-behaved app declares and handles both. Declaring them grants nothing on its own; the code must still request them at run time.

highlights.title: LocationUiState: four states as a sealed type
highlights.explain: The screen is always in exactly one of Idle (nothing asked yet), Loading (acquiring a fix), Success(location), or Error(message). Idle and Loading are data objects with no payload; Success and Error carry data. Because the set is sealed and closed, a 'when' over it is exhaustive and the UI cannot forget a state.

highlights.title: The LocationRepository contract
highlights.explain: One suspend method, currentLocation(), is everything the ViewModel depends on. It is suspend because the real implementation waits for the hardware to produce a fix, and it may throw (no fix, location disabled) - which the ViewModel catches and turns into an Error state. The interface hides whether the coordinate came from GPS or a fake.

highlights.title: Bridging a Play services callback into a suspend call
highlights.explain: getCurrentLocation returns a callback-based Task. suspendCancellableCoroutine turns it into a suspend function: addOnSuccessListener resumes the coroutine with a mapped LocationData (or throws if the fix is null), addOnFailureListener resumes by throwing, and invokeOnCancellation cancels the request via the CancellationTokenSource. @SuppressLint('MissingPermission') is safe here because the UI checks the permission before this ever runs.

highlights.title: The offline fake fix
highlights.explain: FakeLocationRepository implements the same interface with no hardware: it delays briefly (so you can see the Loading spinner) then returns a fixed coordinate - the Googleplex by default. This is what lets the project build, run and unit-test on CI or a bare emulator with no GPS and no Play services, while the permission flow in the UI stays completely real.

highlights.title: The device-vs-fake switch in one place
highlights.explain: provideLocationRepository(context, useFake) is the single decision point. With useFake = true (the default) it returns the offline FakeLocationRepository; flip it to false on a real device (with permission granted) to read actual GPS via RealLocationRepository. The ViewModel depends only on the interface, so nothing else changes.

highlights.title: fetchLocation: Loading, then try/catch
highlights.explain: fetchLocation() launches in viewModelScope, sets Loading, then in a try calls the suspending repository.currentLocation() and publishes Success(location). Any exception - no fix, location off - is caught and becomes Error(message). This single try/catch is the one place a failed fix turns into a visible state instead of a crash. Note there is no fetch in init: it only runs once permission is in hand.

highlights.title: Requesting permissions, then fetching
highlights.explain: The RequestMultiplePermissions launcher requests fine and coarse together; its callback receives a Map<permission, granted>. 'results.values.any { it }' treats ANY granted permission as enough (the user may allow only coarse), updates the hasPermission flag, and either calls viewModel.fetchLocation() on a grant or viewModel.onPermissionDenied() on a refusal - so a denial becomes a visible Error, never a silent no-op.

newVsPrev: WebSocketLive integrated a remote backend over a network socket. LocationServices integrates an on-device API instead - the fused location provider - and adds the piece the networking demos never needed: a real dangerous runtime permission. The new ideas are requesting ACCESS_FINE/COARSE_LOCATION with the RequestMultiplePermissions contract and handling a coarse-only grant, wrapping a callback-based Play services Task into a clean suspend call with suspendCancellableCoroutine, and a ViewModel that deliberately does NOT fetch in init because it must wait for permission. The Real-vs-Fake repository, the sealed UiState, and the StateFlow/collectAsStateWithLifecycle wiring are all the same disciplines carried over from the earlier demos.
tryThis: In LocationRepository.kt set provideLocationRepository's default to useFake = false, run on a device with location enabled, grant the permission, and watch your real latitude/longitude appear instead of the Googleplex coordinate.
On the permission dialog choose 'Only this time' or grant only approximate location, and confirm the app still works because results.values.any treats any granted permission as enough.
Deny the permission and observe the screen show the Error state ('Location permission is required...') via onPermissionDenied(), rather than doing nothing.
In FakeLocationRepository change the fixed coordinate (for example to your city) or increase the delay(600) to delay(3000) to keep the Loading spinner visible for three seconds.
With the real provider, change Priority.PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY to PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY and note how accuracy/battery trade off.
gotchas: The app runs OFFLINE by default (useFake = true): the coordinate is the hardcoded Googleplex fix, not real GPS. The runtime-permission flow is still completely real - only the returned coordinate is faked - so flip the switch to read the actual device location.

@SuppressLint('MissingPermission') on the real getCurrentLocation call is only safe because the UI checks the permission before ever calling fetchLocation. If you call the real repository without a granted permission it will throw a SecurityException.
getCurrentLocation can legitimately resume with a null Location (no fix available yet); the code turns that into an IllegalStateException so it surfaces as the Error state instead of a crash.
RealLocationRepository needs Google Play services on the device. On an emulator image without it, keep useFake = true or set a mock location in the emulator's extended controls.
runTest: Build and run from the project root with ./gradlew installDebug (or ./gradlew assembleDebug to just compile). Launch the app: the status starts NOT GRANTED; tap 'Grant permission & get location' and allow the system dialog, then a brief 'Getting your location...' spinner should give way to a latitude/longitude (the offline Googleplex coordinate by default). To read real GPS, set provideLocationRepository(context, useFake = false) in LocationRepository.kt and run on a device with location enabled. Run the unit tests (fake repository + ViewModel state machine) with ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest - they need no GPS.
